pi_items.source: Task specification; verified as realistic for stage spotlight fixtures (search: stage lighting specifications; ETC, Zumtobel, Litepanels all produce fixtures in this intensity range)
Task specification; verified as standard beam angle for stage ellipsoidal spotlights (theatrical lighting reference: ER spotlights use fixed beam spreads including 40 degrees)
Mathematical calculation using verified formula Φ = 2πI(1 - cos(θ/2)); with I=12000 cd and θ=40°, calculated result is 4547.07 lumens (difference: 0.93 lm or 0.02%)
Task specification; verified as matching tolerance implementation in verifier.py (lines 90-91: lower_bound = expected * 0.95, upper_bound = expected * 1.05)
Cross-verified across multiple sources: (1) task.json description field, (2) export_result.sh script line 10 (RESULT_FILE variable), (3) setup_task.sh cleanup command line 32
Web search for 'SuperSpot 2000' lighting fixture found 'Laser Super Spot 2000' (46,000 lumens overhead LED), but no fixture matching the task specs (12,000 cd, 40° angle = 4,548 lumens)

Verified through multiple authoritative sources (lumencalculator.com, LampHQ candela documentation, compuphase.com). This is the standard formula for converting luminous intensity (candela) and beam angle to luminous flux (lumens).

Real Android app available on Google Play Store; developer: Hassaan; version 2.2.8 (released June 2024); 100K+ downloads; 4MB; includes lighting calculations section (verified through official Google Play listing and multiple APK distribution sites)

privileged_info_summary: The task uses verified engineering parameters: 12,000 candela and 40-degree beam angle are realistic for professional stage spotlights. The expected lumens value of 4548 is mathematically correct per the standard formula Φ = 2πI(1 - cos(θ/2)), with only 0.02% deviation from calculated value. The 5% tolerance matches implementation in verifier.py. The output path /sdcard/spotlight_results.txt is consistently specified across task definition and supporting scripts. The Electrical Calculations Android app (com.hsn.electricalcalculations) is a real, publicly available application with 100K+ downloads. The specific 'SuperSpot 2000' fixture model could not be independently verified; task uses synthetic parameters designed for educational calculation verification rather than real-world fixture data.
